title: SSH Weak Password — Apex Logistics
description: Attack a shared corporate SSH account whose password follows a predictable company-name pattern. Use hydra with a targeted wordlist to recover the credential and retrieve the hidden flag.
outcomes: Explain why company-name based passwords are predictable and dangerous.
Identify the SSH service version and confirm password authentication is enabled.
Use hydra with a targeted corporate wordlist to perform an online dictionary attack.
Log in with recovered credentials and retrieve a sensitive file from the remote host.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Each level builds on the previous one. Do not skip ahead.
* If you get stuck, use the hints — or search for the technique online.
* This exercise is for educational purposes only. Only attack systems within your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a junior penetration tester at SecureWave Ltd. Your client, **Apex Logistics**, runs a freight and warehousing operation. During OSINT you discovered their shift supervisors all share a single SSH account called `shiftlead` on the operations server.

The Apex IT policy document (leaked on a public Pastebin) states that default passwords follow the format *CompanyName + Year*. Your task: build a targeted wordlist, brute-force the SSH service, and retrieve the shift report that contains the flag.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.35.23**, then confirm the target SSH service is reachable:

```
nc -zv 10.1.35.10 22
```

The target operations server is at **10.1.35.10** (SSH port 22). Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
The target SSH service is running locally on port **2222**:

```
nc -zv 127.0.0.1 2222
```

Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Confirm the Target Account
levels.content:
OSINT revealed that Apex Logistics uses a shared SSH account for all shift supervisors. The account name reflects the role — a two-word compound noun describing a senior person running a shift.

Confirm the service is up and accepting password authentication:

```
nmap -p 22 --script ssh-auth-methods --script-args ssh.user=root 10.1.35.10
```

Look for `password` in the auth methods output. If password authentication is enabled, a dictionary attack is viable.

The OSINT also confirmed the account name. It is the job title for someone who **leads** the warehouse **shift** — written as a single lowercase compound word.

**What is the username of the shared shift supervisor account?**
levels.solution:
```
$ nmap -p 22 --script ssh-auth-methods 10.1.35.10
...
|   Supported authentication methods:
|     publickey
|_    password
```

Password authentication is enabled. The shared account is **shiftlead**.

hints.title: What is the account naming convention?
hints.content: The account name is a job title — two words merged into one lowercase compound word. Think about what you call the person who leads a shift.

levels.title: Build a Targeted Wordlist and Crack the Password
levels.content:
The leaked IT policy states passwords follow the pattern **CompanyName + Year**. That means the first word of the password is likely `Apex` (the company name) and the second part is the current or recent year.

A targeted corporate wordlist (`corp-passwords.txt`) has been prepared on your attacker machine based on this intelligence. Run hydra against the SSH service:

```
hydra -l shiftlead -P corp-passwords.txt ssh://10.1.35.10
```

Hydra will try each password in the list against the `shiftlead` account. Because the password follows the leaked policy pattern, it appears in the wordlist and hydra cracks it in seconds.

In a real engagement, you would build this wordlist yourself from OSINT: company name, founding year, product names, location, common suffixes (!, 1, 2024, @).

**What password does hydra recover for the `shiftlead` account?**
levels.solution:
```
$ hydra -l shiftlead -P corp-passwords.txt ssh://10.1.35.10
...
[22][ssh] host: 10.1.35.10   login: shiftlead   password: Apex2024
1 of 1 target successfully completed, 1 valid password found
```

The recovered password is **Apex2024** — exactly the CompanyName + Year pattern the policy described.

hints.title: How do I run hydra against SSH?
hints.content: `hydra -l <username> -P <wordlist> ssh://<target>` runs an online dictionary attack. The `-l` flag sets a single username; `-P` points to the password file.

hints.title: What pattern should the password follow?
hints.content: According to the leaked policy: CompanyName + Year. The company is Apex. Try combinations like Apex2024, Apex2023, Apex1, etc.

hints.title: The exact password
hints.content: The password is `Apex2024` — the company name with the current year appended, no spaces or special characters.

levels.title: Log In and Retrieve the Shift Report
levels.content:
You have the credentials: `shiftlead` / `Apex2024`. Use them to log in over SSH and read the confidential shift report.

```
ssh shiftlead@10.1.35.10
# password: Apex2024
cat ~/shift-report.txt
```

Or in a single non-interactive command:

```
sshpass -p 'Apex2024' ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no shiftlead@10.1.35.10 'cat ~/shift-report.txt'
```

In a real engagement this shared account would give access to the operations server and every shift supervisor's working files — because they all use the same credentials.

**Submit the flag from `shift-report.txt`.**

hints.title: Where is the flag?
hints.content: Log in as `shiftlead` and read `~/shift-report.txt`. The flag is the only line in that file.

hints.title: What format is the flag?
hints.content: The flag starts with `WMG{` and ends with `}`.

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve the game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, had you built a targeted password wordlist from OSINT?

choices.text: I understood the concept but not tried

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What would you change or add to improve this game?
